meta.setting: Ranch fence lines, stock trails, water holes, and road edges around French Gulch
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with range travel, reputation, animal handling, local law, and hard choices

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Northern California mining commerce and road-town context that also supports ranch and freight pressure.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Deer
research_grounding.use: Deer movement across California as part of fence, forage, predator, and hunting context.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Fence Riders cast page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: square image reference immediately after the title
opening blockquote with invented range note, staple warning, or cut-wire saying generated later
H2 archetype subtitle in the register of Wire and Boundary Men
one or two compact lore paragraphs introducing them as range workers who patrol fence breaks, stock drift, water rights, and grudges
H3 Role with a short table-facing function phrase
H3 Traits with two to four terse signs or habits
10 H4 headings, each followed later by a brief playable move, leverage, cost, edge, or complication
H3 Camp Say closing blockquote with plain meaning generated later
generation_guidance: Make fence riders weary mediators between ranch claims, mining roads, tribal land pressure, hungry cattle, and men who pretend a wire line is the same as justice.
Tie future moves to cut wire, stock drift, boundary testimony, water access, night watch, fence repair, hidden tracks, and practical revenge.
Keep them morally mixed: a rider can protect a widow's herd and still serve a cruel owner before sundown.
Use barbed wire, bent staples, saddle sores, gate chains, hoof churn, dust, and water holes as table-facing objects.
Do not include final move names or final move text in this JSON.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: Write them with saddle stiffness and a pocket full of bent staples. A fence line is never just a line; it is a rumor nailed to posts.
generation_prompt.table_spark: A fence-rider scene should ask who cut the wire, who benefits from loose stock, and who gets to call a line lawful.
must_preserve: Keep boundary conflict practical and social, not symbolic only.
The generated page must include image path images/fence-riders.jpg.
No finished page prose, final moves, or final sayings belong in this prompt file.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not include medieval, enchanted, sacred, or fake-tribal framing
do not make fences clean legal certainties; they should create disputes
do not use modern slang, modern gear, vehicles, or clean theme-park staging
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about fence work, boundary pressure, stock, water, and table action.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
